summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates an unconventional use of Docker: running desktop applications in containers on Linux. Motivated by the desire for OS X-style application sandboxing and clean uninstalls, she shows how to containerize everything from text-based apps (IRC, email, Twitter clients) to full GUI applications (Chrome, Spotify, Skype, Tor Browser) by mounting the X11 socket. This approach provides resource control (CPU/memory limits), application isolation, and the satisfaction of knowing deleted apps leave no traces behind.
key_points: Docker can run GUI applications by mounting the X11 socket rather than using SSH X11 forwarding
Containerizing desktop apps provides resource control - you can limit CPU and memory usage
This approach ensures clean uninstalls with no leftover files scattered across the system
The technique works for everything from simple TUI apps to complex GUI apps requiring sound and video
money_quotes: I use Docker to run all the desktop apps on my computers.
I hate installing things on my host and the files getting everywhere. I wanted the ability to delete an app and know it is gone fully without some random file hanging around.
Yes, the cpu/memory hungry chrome is now perfectly contained!
Because why should you ever have to install ssh into a container? EWWW UNNECESSARY BLOAT!
The images work by mounting the X11 socket into the container! Yippeeeee!
key_insight: Docker containers aren't just for production deployments—they can sandbox desktop applications, giving you resource control and clean isolation that traditional package managers can't provide.
